data.title: Rimini Street: Strategic Growth And Margin Strength
data.description:
Rimini Street (NASDAQ:RMNI) reported mixed Q1 FY25 results, with a small YoY revenue decline related to their gradual shut down of their PeopleSoft support business.

However, I believe the market is discounting the future growth opportunities this company has in store. Rimini is evolving to support a host of modern software solutions, and they are also doing so much more profitably than they have been able to in the past.

Rimini's partnerships with critical providers of enterprise tools is a powerful tailwind that I believe positions the company to generate substantial returns for investors over the next few years.

The stock appears meaningfully undervalued at the moment given their opportunities to scale, along with their recurring revenue model and improving margins. Given this context, I am initiating a BUY rating on Rimini Street based on the potential upside and the growing relevance of their business model.

Business Description & Fundamentals

Rimini Street's primary objective is to help companies extend the useful life of certain software systems (such as those offered by Oracle and SAP) through Rimini's affordable support offerings. Many vendors, including those listed above, do not continue to support older versions of their software offerings years after release, which forces customers to upgrade (whether they want to or not).

Rimini gives customers the option to avoid these costly and often disruptive upgrades by providing third party support for things like bug fixes, security updates, performance optimization, and system monitoring.

The Rimini ONE platform includes several layers such as Application Management Services (AMS) for overall system management, Third-Party Software Support to help customers avoid paying vendors for maintenance and upgrade support, and Interoperability Services through Rimini Connect which helps customers integrate older systems with modern cloud and hybrid cloud environments.

Rimini's primary offering is designed to help customers avoid upgrading for around 15 years, which frees up corporate IT budgets for other projects that can offer substantially higher returns on investment.

Rimini currently has 3,092 active clients as of the end of the first quarter, which was up from 3,040 a year ago. However, this figure is a little deceptive as it counts clients who've purchased more than one product multiple times. Their unique client count is currently 1,575, up from 1,535 a year ago. Rimini also has a plethora of large businesses as customers, with 71 Fortune 500 companies and 19 Fortune Global 100 companies, which I believe demonstrates the value they can provide to large organizations.

One of the biggest growth drivers for Rimini moving forward is their new partnership with ServiceNow (NOW). Rimini already assists clients with both onboarding and integrating with ServiceNow's platform as it can be tricky to set up on top of legacy ERP systems like Oracle or SAP. As part of this new partnership, ServiceNow is training its sales people to include Rimini in their go-to-market strategy, which should expand Rimini's reach to new customers.

The deal makes perfect sense as Rimini enables substantial cost savings for businesses in regards to their legacy systems, which frees up IT budgets to spend on more modern software tools (such as ServiceNow's platform for workflow automation). Rimini's CEO Seth Ravin noted on the earning call that this is one of the company's largest long term growth opportunities, and it makes sense to me given the synergies between the two companies.

Rimini has also joined forces with Workday (WDAY) and Rimini is now considered a certified application management services provider for them. This allows Rimini to support clients not just on older systems, but also on more modern platforms like Workday where Rimini can aid customers in lowering costs and improving system performance. Additionally, hundreds of Rimini's customers already use Workday so there is a natural upsell opportunity here.

Support sales related to VMware also spiked this past quarter, with many small and medium sized businesses turning to Rimini to avoid the huge price increases tied to VMware's new subscription based business model. This change came largely as a result of Broadcom's acquisition of VMware back in 2022. Following these changes, some companies saw support costs associated with VMware jump over 400%, and Rimini offers a much more affordable alternative which allows firms to continue using VMware infrastructure efficiently.

There are also a couple of risks that are worth monitoring when it comes to the fundamentals of Rimini's business. First off, macroeconomic uncertainty this past quarter has led to a decline in revenue retention as this figure declined 1% YoY to 88%. According to management, some customers chose not to renew their subscriptions due to both geopolitical tensions and a shift in their IT budget priorities towards other efforts (such as AI tools).

However, management is looking at this situation optimistically as they believe that if and when budgets tighten further, more IT departments will begin to view Rimini's low cost support model as an attractive alternative to paying for direct vendor support. Additionally, during past downturns (such as the Dot-Com bubble and the GFC) many customers stayed with Rimini through the lows, demonstrating the resilient nature of their business model.

One of the biggest risk factors for Rimini since the company went public has been the litigation risk from Oracle and other vendors they are undercutting on support prices. The Rimini ONE case has officially been resolved, however the Rimini II case is still an ongoing concern. So far, the courts have ruled that Oracle is not entitled to any financial damages, and Rimini is also actively pursuing recovery of $58.7 million worth of attorney's fees from Oracle.

However, management has suspended their guidance partially due to this legal risk which means it is something that investors should monitor. However, I believe this case is unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the company's financials at any point in the future.

Financials

Revenue in Q1 FY25 was down 2% YoY to $104.2 million compared to $106.7 million a year ago. This decline was primarily due to the slow wind down of Rimini's Oracle PeopleSoft support business which contributed only $7 million in revenue (7% of the total) compared to $8.2 million (8% of the total) a year ago.

The choice to exit this business has been debated extensively by executives at Rimini, however they reaffirmed their commitment to shutting this unit down in their most recent 10-Q filing. The rationale here makes sense given this segment supports a legacy product that itself is experiencing a structural decline in demand. Also, Rimini's legal battles with Oracle related to this product have been a major overhang on the stock for a number of years, which is another reason management is choosing to walk away.

While revenue declined, net income more than doubled compared to Q1 FY24, hitting $3.4 million this past quarter. This change was primarily due to improved operating cost controls and lower legal expenses compared to the prior year period. ARR was slightly disappointing, coming in at $396 million compared to $416 million a year ago. Management attributed this decline to higher customer churn as well as the decline in the PeopleSoft business.

Profitability metrics across the board were very strong, with gross margins improving 120bps YoY to 61%, mostly due to a $2.2 million decline in compensation expenses tied to delivering their services to customers. S&M expenses fell 12% YoY to $34.3 million, and this cost section now only accounts for 33% of revenue (a 400bps improvement YoY).

G&A expenses also declined 5% to $17.5 million, however management expects this line item to increase in Q2 FY25 due to investments in growth opportunities and higher regulatory costs.

Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter hit $15.3 million, which let to a substantial adjusted EBITDA margin increase of 470bps YoY. Billings, which are a forward looking indicator for companies with subscription business models, increased 7.2% YoY to $79.4 million. Additionally, if you exclude the decline in the PeopleSoft business, billings actually grew 11.3% YoY. This demonstrates strength in Rimini's new core suite of offerings, and I view this as an incredibly strong sign for the future of the company.

Finally, the company's liquidity position remains very solid. Rimini ended the quarter with $123.8 million in cash and cash equivalents and a net cash position of $27.1 million. While they are currently running a working capital deficit of ~$60 million, this is almost entirely due to their high current deferred revenue balance. Given deferred revenue acts as a source of cash in the near term, I view their balance sheet as very strong and this also gives Rimini the flexibility to offer great terms to customers to increase retention rates if we experience an economic downturn.

Valuation

In order to determine Rimini Street's intrinsic value, I created a discounted cash flow model. The perpetual growth rate used in the model was 2%, and my projection period covers the next 5 years. Also, the figures for cash, debt, FY24 financials, and shares outstanding were all pulled from S&P Capital IQ.

For my weighted average cost of capital I first computed the cost of debt using the company's TTM interest expense divided by the total debt balance as of the end of the most recent quarter. I then calculated the cost of equity using the yield on a 10-year U.S. treasury bond as my risk free rate, the stock's beta from Capital IQ, and the equity risk published on Professor Aswath Damodaran's website for the month of June.

My revenue growth rates were conservative, as I have projected a slight decline over each of the next two years to reflect the PeopleSoft wind down. After FY26 I have the top line returning to growth of between 1%-2% annually to reflect growth from the new partnerships with ServiceNow, Workday, and others.

For operating margins, I have modeled in no changes as the company is actively investing in new growth opportunities which may offset some of their cost controls they have implemented recently. I also assumed there will be very minimal taxes due over the projection period due to Rimini's $235 million accumulated deficit and the large NOLs they can utilize over the coming years.

For the cash flow statement items, I have D&A expenses declining modestly over the next few years. My reasoning here is that capital expenditures have been very low over the past 5 years, at around 0.9% of revenue annually, and so D&A should naturally follow suit. I also modeled the change in net working capital to be around 2% sales, which reflects recent trends as this figure has been positive over the last few years.

Based on these assumptions I arrived at an equity value of ~$612 million and an implied share price of $6.66 after dividing the equity value by Rimini's fully diluted shares outstanding. This represents sizable upside of around 97.8% based on the current market share price of $3.37 as of Thursday, June 12th.

Conclusion

While some of Rimini's financial results for the first quarter were underwhelming, I believe their most recent report underscored strength in a number of key areas. Between margin expansion, strong free cash flow generation, and progress related to key partnerships with powerful enterprise software providers, there is a lot to like here.

I believe the company's role as a cost saving engine for businesses with both legacy and modern IT infrastructure is a great niche to be operating in at the moment, especially given the current uncertainty regarding the economic outlook over the coming quarters.

Both the ServiceNow and Workday deals will likely allow Rimini to add a number of new clients, which should boost ARR and in turn the top and bottom line moving forward. Although there are still some risks related to the economy, customer retention, and the Oracle lawsuit, I believe they are minimal relative to the potential catalysts on the horizon.

My valuation model also implies that shares are worth substantially more than their current market value based on the present value of expected future cash flows. While these assumptions may not play out perfectly in reality, I cannot imagine shares stay in this range for much longer. I am very bullish on this stock at the moment, and therefore I am rating the stock a BUY.

data.title: Diamondback Energy: U.S. Oil Export Power Shines As Strait Of Hormuz Concerns Rise
data.description:
Diamondback Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:FANG) opened up around 4% higher Friday morning, while the price of crude oil futures (CL1:COM) was up around 8%. Crude oil futures are currently up around 13% this week, making it one of the commodity's best weeks in recent years, back to about $73 per barrel. This spike came after Israel launched a strike on Iranian military and nuclear targets. Although President Trump has since pushed Iran to make a deal, the risk of escalation appears significant, as both sides consider various forms of retaliation and pressure.

Historically, I've seen Diamondback Energy as a hedge against this situation. I covered it last August in "Diamondback Energy: Increased Geopolitical Tensions Raise Free Cash Flow Outlook." My core view was that North American production giants like Diamondback have a specific edge because the ability of Europe and Asia to export and transport oil through the Strait of Hormuz safely is compromised or potentially at risk. In such a scenario, North American producers like Diamondback could lead the way in exports at a potentially much higher price.

That said, since economic growth factors have generally slowed since last year, the price of oil has declined. FANG is down approximately 20% since I last covered it, while oil prices were down about $5, but have since returned to about even since last August. FANG's valuation remains slightly low with a forward "P/E" of 11X and a forward dividend yield of 3.6%.

In my opinion, the company has an excellent track record of prioritizing shareholder value through accretive acquisitions and strategic development opportunities. Since last August, Diamondback's outlook has changed following its significant merger with Endeavor Energy. On the one hand, this deal has increased Diamondback's leverage while also improving its upside potential to higher energy commodity prices. Although we can still only speculate on the matter, I suspect that FANG may face a sharp Bull market if supply-side risk factors continue to arise, particularly geopolitical risks.

Understanding Oil's Hedge Potential

My position on FANG and oil in general is fundamentally a hedge against risks related primarily to the Middle East. This was explained in my final takeaway:

In my view, US producers, with their innate capacity to export abroad and lack of political risk (compared to the Middle East), make them an ideal portfolio hedge against oil price risk. Remember, a geopolitical oil price shock would hamper the economy while raising inflation, meaning stocks and bonds may decline in that scenario while tying up the Federal Reserve's ability to stimulate the economy. Thus, stocks like FANG are among the few that should offer strong hedges against this risk.

The need for such could not be more clear today. If oil prices rise sharply, not due to strong economic demand, but to a potentially massive decline in supply stability, we should see a combined rise in inflation and economic strain. However, if oil prices rise, pushing inflation higher, the Federal Reserve's ability to provide dovish quantitative easing stimulus may also be compromised. In such a scenario, I suspect that oil producers like FANG may be among the few equities to rise.

To me, the opportunity in FANG and other North American oil producers is not necessarily a means of increasing performance, but a unique hedge against unique risk that would be bearish for almost all other equity market segments. FANG is a hedge because the oil price exposure to geopolitical conflicts is highly asymmetrical. Iran exports around 1.3M barrels per day, or just over 1% of global consumption. Saudi Arabia accounts for approximately 6.6M, while the UAE is around 2.6M, Qatar around 1.5M, Iraq about 6.2M, and Oman is approximately 900K.

In my view, there is relatively high potential that some of Iran's oil infrastructure will be compromised. If the conflict escalates significantly, the Strait of Hormuz may close, threatening the ~30% of all seaborne crude that flows through the strait. Some of that capacity may be diverted, but not likely the majority of it. That would likely result in the most significant global oil supply shock ever, a similar decline to the forced cuts seen in 2020, but without a direct and considerable decrease in demand.

Furthermore, the U.S. currently lacks a substantial Strategic Petroleum Reserve, having depleted much of it to lower oil prices in 2022, thereby increasing the market's vulnerability to an oil supply crisis. Since the world would essentially become oil-price takers, unable to reduce consumption quickly, it is expected that oil prices could rise to $200 per barrel.

We must keep in mind that oil and gas are cheap today. Oil rose to nearly $150 in 2008 when consumer prices were significantly lower. The inflation-adjusted oil price is now back around its lowest level, excluding the COVID extreme, over the past twenty years:

At the 2008 peak prices, oil would be around $220 today, adjusting for inflation. Arguably, because oil production technology has improved over the past two decades, the cost of production has not risen as fast as inflation, meaning such a price is not necessarily likely. However, it remains true that oil is very cheap, enough that oil producers are not expected to profit significantly on newly drilled wells, even in the cheap and abundant Permian basin.

As a result of low prices, most producers in the U.S. and abroad are dramatically slowing output growth by slowing well drilling. U.S. domestic production remains very high, but has stagnated and may be peaking as drilling levels are so low, falling more sharply after the market correction two months ago:

At the least, I suspect that lower drilling activity limits Diamondback and other energy companies' exposure to a recessionary slowdown in oil demand. Unlike past slowdowns, oil producers appear to be very quick to reduce capital expenditures on expansion, concerned that the market may revert to a glut. Still, I suspect that drilling levels are unsustainably low, while I am not convinced that oil and gas demand will drop significantly in a recession. Keep in mind that improvements in vehicle mileage partly drove the 2008 decline in demand, while the 2020 decline in oil demand was due to lockdowns that directly limited global transportation. I think it's fair to assume U.S. oil and gas demand will not rise, but there are limited signals that it will decline soon.

Diamondback Growing Through Accretion

Like most oil and gas producers, Diamondback has scaled back its organic growth through drilling and exploration since the late 2010s. Its Capex-to-revenue remains elevated and did rise after its recent acquisition, but the company is not growing nearly as quickly through those means. See below:

Diamondback's growth has shifted from drilling and exploration to accretive acquisitions. In general, larger oil companies with greater economic scale have higher "P/E" valuations today than smaller exploration and development companies, creating accretive acquisition growth opportunities. Diamondback's revenue has risen dramatically over recent years, even adjusting for the rise in energy commodity prices. Its debt has also increased, but has remained proportional to its revenue growth:

During its last investor call, Diamondback's managers emphasized their efforts to slow down capital expenditures, aligning with the efforts of most other producers to derisk due to economic demand concerns. Its dividend will fall with prices in the sub-$60 range, but that is less of an issue now that oil is back above $70. Still, the company remains focused on using cash flows to reduce leverage and pursue share repurchases. Crucially, the call mentions that Diamondback holds the most considerable drilled-but-uncompleted well backlog in the Permian Basin, enabling it to maintain production by completing wells without incurring costly drilling expenses.

The "DUC" backlog across all U.S. oil producers was huge going into 2020. After the crash in production, many relied on these wells as a low-cost way of improving production. DUC well inventories are likely a significant reason why oil production has remained flat over the past two years, despite significantly lower drilling levels. However, the inventory of these wells was very low going into 2025, while the rig count, even in the Permian, continued to decline:

In my view, the combination of low drilled-but-uncompleted well inventories and lower drilling activity indicates a potential decline in U.S. production. This is generally anticipated to offset an expected economic-driven decrease in US. oil demand, but for now, we're not seeing that and may not, depending on tariff policies. In my view, it is most likely that U.S. oil demand, as well as global demand in general, will remain relatively stagnant, while various factors, including oil production cuts and a potentially extreme decline in Middle Eastern supply availability, may push supply levels significantly lower.

Diamondback is ideally suited for this scenario, as it can raise production quickly if prices rise. Diamondback is, to me, slightly undervalued, assuming oil and gas remain around current levels that ensure profitability. Being a U.S. company with vast reserves, I continue to see Diamondback having a huge potential upside from geopolitical risk factors that may significantly increase global demand for U.S. crude exports. Many other North American E&Ps also meet the criteria; however, combined with Diamondback's scale advantage and long history of shareholder value approach, it provides the best risk-reward profile among E&Ps.

The Bottom Line

We cannot know the odds of the Strait of Hormuz shutting down. Still, for me, the fact that we've seen a long-term escalation in Middle Eastern-related geopolitical issues over several years makes this scenario more likely than the market is currently anticipating. Ultimately, because this risk factor is bearish for most stocks and bonds, I think investors are best off focusing on the few equities with hedge potential. Although there are many E&Ps, Diamondback has, to me, the ideal tradeoff between stability and scalability potential while focusing more squarely on shareholder value.

Of course, there are other ways to hedge against the risk of a significant, geopolitically related rise in oil prices. The United States Oil Fund, LP ETF (USO) is a decent option, but it lacks the dividend potential of FANG. As oil is not currently far from breakeven, a substantial increase in oil prices would significantly improve its EPS. Diamondback's income outlook slid with lower profit margin expectations. Likely, with oil prices back up over $70 today, this will improve. See below:

Regarding the potential for the Strait of Hormuz to shut down, it is unclear if that is likely, and it's reasonable to assume it is not the most likely outcome. Some disruption to Middle Eastern oil supplies seems relatively likely given that Iran's oil facilities are viewed as a target. That said, even if it's less than 50% likely that a severe disruption scenario occurs, the potential upside in oil (which would slow the global economy) is likely to be far more extreme than the $5-$10 downside if the conflict is peacefully resolved.

The other risk to my bullish outlook on Diamondback is a severe economic slowdown. This is a widely anticipated risk factor, and one that Diamondback and its peers are preparing for by dramatically lowering CapEx and drilling activity. In my view, most E&Ps are overestimating the decline in oil demand that could occur if the economy falters. The decrease in the rig count is almost akin to that seen in early 2020, which, of course, was not driven by the economy, but by a direct global effort to reduce transportation activity. I suspect ongoing drilling reductions limit oil's recession exposure and may be sufficient to create an oil shortage regardless of geopolitical events.

Still, as we saw earlier this year, FANG is very likely to decline if the equity market crashes. However, unlike previous declines, I expect oil producers to prove less cyclical than assumed, with a potentially significant inflation hedge benefit.

data.title: Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce: Recent Gains Leave Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
For all the economic uncertainty facing the Canadian and U.S. economies, Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (NYSE:CM, TSX:CM:CA) ("CIBC") has done okay since my opening Buy-rated piece last August. On the business front, interest margin expansion and elevated trading revenue have helped offset a modest uptick in provisioning, driving double-digit growth in the bank's profits. That performance has also worked its way through to the shares, with CIBC returning around 24% in native currency terms in that time. That performance is enough to place CIBC near the top-end of its Big Six peers – roughly in line with Toronto Dominion (NYSE:TD) (TSX:TD:CA), and trailing only Bank of Montreal (NYSE:BMO) (TSX:BMO:CA).

Unsurprisingly, CIBC's share price growth has outpaced the growth in its dividend and book value, leaving the stock more expensive than it was when I first covered it. I still like CIBC on a broad level - particularly its highly profitable Canadian business - but there is less value in the shares than there was.

Earnings Are Up, But Credit Weakening

(All financial figures in Canadian dollars unless stated otherwise.)

CIBC's headline numbers have developed nicely in the three quarters since my opening piece. Revenue is up around 6%, pre-tax profit around 7%, and, helped by buybacks, earnings per share by roughly 12%.

Not all of CIBC's business units have rowed in the same direction. The core Canadian retail, commercial and wealth activities netted it $1.3 billion in profit last quarter, up modestly on my first piece. This is an excellent business making a 20%-plus return on equity.

CIBC's Capital Markets business has also clocked in strong growth, with net income nearly doubling to $566 million in that time, aided by elevated trading revenue. In contrast, its U.S. retail & commercial bank has been a bit weaker, with quarterly profits slipping on the back of higher loan loss provisioning.

Net interest income accounts for around 53% of CIBC's revenue. Policy rates have fallen 100bps and 175bps in the U.S. and Canada, respectively, since I first covered the stock, and like many banks, CIBC's funding costs have fallen more than the income it generates from its assets. This has helped fuel modest expansion in its lending margin and its net interest income. Elsewhere, recent volatility seems to have been a boon for its trading business, with trading revenue landing at around $750 million last quarter, up nearly 70% on my opener.

While operating expenses have also risen, CIBC's cost growth has trailed its top line. That has driven a circa 130bps improvement in the bank's cost/income ratio, with quarterly pre-provision pretax income up nearly 10% in that time to $3.2 billion.

Turning to credit quality, this has taken on even more salience recently given the ever-changing situation with U.S. tariffs. CIBC's gross impaired loans ratio continues to tick up modestly, landing at 0.57% last quarter, and the bank is setting aside more to cover bad debt, booking a circa $605 million credit charge last quarter, up around 25% on my opening piece. Relative to total loans, this line has risen by around 9bps to 0.44%.

CIBC has modestly increased its allowance for credit losses to the equivalent of 0.75% of total loans. As its first line of defense, it is generating around $13 billion in annual pre-provision pretax income to absorb credit-related charges. This is equal to around 2.3% of its total loans.

Management has also prudently bolstered the bank's capital position. CIBC now sports a strong 13.4% Common Equity Tier 1 ratio, which is 190bps over its regulatory requirement. Management aims to preserve an additional 100bps buffer on top of this. That would imply around $3.1 billion of surplus capital, equal to 3.6% of CIBC's current market-cap.

Thanks to its strong domestic business, CIBC earns good quality profits overall, with last quarter's net income mapping to a 13.8% return on equity. This is a little below management's 15% target, although surplus capital is weighing on this metric. Buybacks will help raise this.

Relative to risk weighted assets, CIBC is earning around 230bps annually, implying strong capital generation. This allows the bank to fund a decent dividend, organic growth, and supplemental returns via buybacks, all while maintaining a robust capital cushion. CIBC has paid out $2.67 billion in common stock dividends since my opener, while shares outstanding have declined by around 10 million, or roughly 1%. Book value per share has risen by over 7%, and its CET1 ratio is up 10bps.

Multiples Above Recent Averages

At its current $92.93 price in Toronto trading, CIBC has risen around 20% since my first piece. This has outpaced the growth in its quarterly dividend, up 8% to $0.97, and its per-share book value, up 7% to $59.65.

As a result, CIBC's multiples have become slightly more expensive. The annualized dividend yield is now around 4.2%, down 50bps, while CIBC's price/book multiple is 1.56x, up around 0.18x. Comparing those to recent averages, the forward dividend yield is now over 100bps below its 5-year average, while its P/B ratio represents a circa 0.25x premium to the same.

I wouldn't say that makes the stock expensive as such. Based on its current return on equity, CIBC trades for around 11.5x earnings. Its dividend only consumes 45%-50% of net income, meaning the bank has plenty of scope to carry on growing its payout. All else equal, a 9%-10% annualized return requires CIBC to meet its historical average rate of dividend growth (~5.40% annualized).

Conclusion

There has always been a lot to like about CIBC on a broad level. It operates a highly profitable retail & business bank in Canada, and like its peers, the bank sports an incredible distribution record: CIBC has not skipped a dividend since it first paid one in 1868. Its shares aren't necessarily expensive, offering investors a 4.2% dividend yield on a modest sub-50% payout ratio. Growth in line with the firm's historical average can drive a respectable result from here. However, with CIBC's multiples less attractive than they were when I opened on it, the valuation is a bit less appealing than it was.

data.title: SPDR ICE Preferred Securities ETF's Portfolio Review
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

In the following lines, we'll present to you the holdings of the SPDR® ICE Preferred Securities ETF (NYSEARCA:PSK), mainly concentrated in fixed preferred stocks.

Fund Overview

At the end of the first quarter of 2025, PSK had close to $806 million in assets, distributed in 156 holdings. The 10-year total return of the fund is 2.45%, the average yield to worst is 6.32%, and the current yield is 5.50%. The credit quality score is around the Baa2-Baa3 range.

The holdings

Fixed-rate preferred stocks>PAR

The fund's fixed-rate preferred stocks that trade above par represent approximately 9% of the portfolio. You can see them in the pictures below:

Still not callable:

We present them by their yield to call and years to call:

Already Callable:

We display them based on their current yield and stripped price, allowing for an assessment of potential call risk by observing how far they trade from par.

And their credit score:

Fixed-rate preferred stocks<PAR

Fixed-rate preferred stocks, which trade below par, make up 46% of the portfolio. Their weighted average current yield is 6.42%, and their average credit rating is Baa3. You can see them in the charts below.

Fixed-rate bonds < PAR

Fixed-rate bonds, which trade below par, make up 23% of the portfolio. They have a weighted average yield to worst of 7.05% and an average weighted maturity of 41.45 years. Their average credit rating is Baa3. You can see them in the chart below.

Credit scores:

Fixed-rate bonds >PAR

Those fixed-rate bonds that are below par represent 2% of the portfolio. They have an average weighted yield to call of 6.59% and average weighted years to call of 3.50. Their average-weighted credit score is Baa2. You can see them in the chart below:

Fixed to floating > PAR

Our approach to valuing floating or resettable preferred shares simplifies the calculation of expected returns by replacing them with fixed-rate instruments based on the current swap rate. We assume that this interest rate will remain constant after the redemption date. For the calculations, we use a final period of 40 years, and on this basis, we determine the XIRR. For easier interpretation in the charts, we also calculate the expected duration of the corresponding fixed-rate instrument.

Fixed-to-floating preferred shares are tied to LIBOR (SOFR + 0.26%). Those that trade above par constitute 8% of the portfolio. Their weighted average current yield is 7.83%, their future weighted rate is 8.07%, and their average price is 26.70. Their yield to call is negative. The average credit rating is Baa3.

Fixed to floating <PAR

Fixed to floating stocks below par are 4% of the VRP's portfolio. Their average weighted current yield is 6.44%, average weighted future rate is 8.41%, and average weighted price is 22.88. The average credit rating is Baa3

Floor rates

Floor rates stocks are roughly 3 % of the portfolio. Their average weighted current yield is 6.06%, average weighted future rate is 5.43%, and average weighted price is 21.71. The average credit rating is Baa2.

Resettable>PAR

The resettable stocks that trade above par represent 3% of the portfolio. Their average weighted current yield is 6.84%, average weighted future rate is 8.01%, and average weighted price is 25.49. The average weighted yield to call is 6.45%, and the average credit rating is Baa3.

You can see them in the picture below:

Resettable<PAR

Those stocks that are resettable and trade below PAR are 2% of the portfolio. Their average weighted current yield is 6.34%, average weighted future rate is 6.27%, and average weighted price is 24.69. The average credit rating is Baa3.

Credit ratings of all resettable stocks:

Finally, we provide you with a chart with all PSK's holdings that have a floating rate, floor rate, and resettables, and their calculated XIRR averages:

Conclusion

When you buy PSK, you're buying the following:

  • 46% fixed-rate preferred stocks < PAR with a credit rating of Baa3, current yield 6.42%, and an average price of $19.58
  • 9% fixed-rate preferred stocks > PAR, credit rating Baa3, current yield 6.97%, and price of $25.64
  • 23% fixed-rate bonds < PAR with a credit rating Baa3, yield to worst 7.05%, maturity of 41.45 years, and a price of $20.42.
  • 2% fixed-rate bonds > PAR with a credit rating Baa2, yield to call 6.59%, 3.5 years to call, and an average price of $25.43.
  • 8% fixed-to-floating preferred stocks > PAR tied to LIBOR (SOFR + 0.26%) with a credit rating Baa3, current yield 7.83%, future rate 8.07%, and average price $26.70.

data.title: CoreCivic: A Clear Beneficiary Of The Current Administration's Tough Immigration Policies
data.description:
Introduction

Based on my analysis, even though CoreCivic's (NYSE:CXW) share price has increased by 87.64% in the past year, I believe that the company is still undervalued. Apart from potential revenue growth due to the Trump administration's more aggressive immigration policies, CXW has the potential to return more value to its shareholders by maintaining financial discipline. My valuation analysis suggests that there is a potential upside of 18% for investors to capitalize on. In this report, I will demonstrate why investors should consider CXW in their portfolios.

Company Overview

CXW provides a range of solutions in the areas of corrections and detention in the United States. CXW operations can be categorized into the following three segments: (1) Safety, (2) Community, and (3) Properties. As of 1Q25, the company is one of the largest prison operators in the US, operating 43 detention facilities with a total capacity of 62k beds. Additionally, through CXW's community and properties segment, the company owns 21 residential re-entry centers and 6 correctional related properties that represents about 4k and 10k beds respectively.

Recent Developments

In the most recent quarter, CXW generated $488.63 million in revenue, representing a year-on-year decline of 2.41%. Revenue decline is primarily due to California City's contract expiration in the property segment. That being said, CXW has signed a new contract with California City; by 2Q25, California City should resume contribution to CXW's topline in the company's safety segment.

Margins for the period have improved as compared to the same period last year. It is important to note that the expansion in the company's margins should not be attributable to an improvement in operating efficiency. Due to higher OPEX, operating margin for the period fell to 9.69% from 10.86% in the same period last year. However, because of lower financial related expenses, net income surged by 163% year-on-year while net income margin improved to 5.14% from 1.91% in the same period last year.

Looking ahead, CXW remains highly optimistic for FY2025. In fact, the company recently revised guidance towards the upside. CXW increased its net income guidance by 59.17%; for the year, CXW expects to generate a net income of $96.3 million. Separately, CXW also revised its diluted EPS and diluted FFO per share higher by 60.55% and 23.34%; for the year, CXW expects to post a diluted EPS and diluted FFO per share of $0.87 and $1.77.

Aggressive US Administration Will Continue to Fuel Demand for CXW's Services

Thus far, the Trump administration has been extremely aggressive in its immigration policies. In fact, ICE had set a target to increase the number of arrests to 3k a day. As of June 2025, the total detained population by ICE had increased from 39.24k to 47.93k, growing at an average rate of 4% per month; this clearly indicates that the administration is not just talking and pushing papers without actions.

It is likely that we will see the administration further tightening its immigration policies. As a reference, Tom Homan, Director of ICE, had recently stated that there is a need to expand the capacity of detention facilities by almost 2x to 100k beds. Considering the current trend and growth rate of 4% a month, it will take less than 20 months for ICE to fully utilize 100k beds.

Naturally, as one of the biggest service provider to ICE, CXW will be a key beneficiary of the administration's aggressive policies. In fact, CXW had already won multiple contracts with ICE in 1Q25. Overall, the total cumulative capacity of these contracts is approximately 6.77k beds, suggesting that there is still space for more contracts with ICE down the road.

Based on CXW's current utilization rate and capacity, the company's revenue per bed in a quarter is approximately $8.3k. Therefore, the additional 6.77k beds will bring in roughly $56.52 million in revenue for one quarter (i.e., 12% of total revenue in 1Q25). This is substantial because it represents an additional $226 million per year. As a reference, CXW generated $1.96 billion in FY2024.

Overall, as long as ICE and the current administration remains aggressive, it is reasonable to believe that CXW will continue to see strong demand for their services. In fact, CXW's recent decision to increase its FY2025 capital expenditures to $67 million from $42.5 million suggests that the company believes that their current capacity will not adequately fulfil the subsequent rise in demand.

Improving Debt Profile Will Allow CXW to Return More Value to Shareholders

Since the start of 2024, CXW has been reducing their total debt at an average rate of 3% per quarter; total debt fell by more than 10% within five quarters to $982 million. Currently, the company does not have any substantial debt maturities to worry about. Moreover, CXW's total cumulative maturities for the next three years is $256 million. Given that the company has a cash reserve of $107 million and the ability to generate more than $150 million in free cash flow per year, CXW can easily meet its maturities for the next three years.

More importantly, CXW's net income margin will continue expanding as long as the company continues to pay down its maturities and not take on substantial debt. Since 1Q24, net income margin has substantially expanded by 2.69x from 1.91% to 5.14% in the latest quarter. Margin expansion will also improve the company's cash flow profile, allowing CXW to return more value to shareholders.

Previously, in March 2022, CXW's board of directors had approved a share repurchase plan that allows the company to repurchase up to $350 million worth of shares. As of 1Q25, CXW had already repurchased $219 million worth of shares, indicating that the company can still repurchase shares worth up to $131 million; this is approximately 5.6% of the company's current market capitalization.

Valuation Analysis Suggests That CXW Is Still Underpriced

To ascertain the value of CXW, I have conducted a DCF-based valuation analysis. Even though the company's share price has increased by 87.64% in the past year, the results of my model suggest that CXW is still undervalued. My model is based on the following assumptions and reasons:

  1. Revenue to grow at a CAGR of 5% per annum. This assumption accounts the increase in demand for CXW's services due to more aggressive immigration policies. Given that CXW's new contracts with ICE may potentially bring in an additional $226 million, it should be fairly easy for CXW to achieve a 5% revenue growth rate.
  2. Net margin to maintain at 5.14% through the next five years. CXW should be able to maintain its net margins easily given that the company has proved to expand it margins in the recent quarters by reducing debt.
  3. Terminal growth of 2% and WACC of 7.73%.

Based on these assumptions and CXW's outstanding shares of 110.46 million, my model suggests that CXW's share price should be worth $25. Currently, CXW trades at $21.19, indicating an upside potential of 18%. It is important to note that the assumptions used are fairly conservative and are significantly lower than what CXW can potentially achieve.

Potential Risks & Closing Remarks

Overall, CXW represents a good buying opportunity for investors who want to capitalize on the current administration's aggressive immigration policies. Apart from potential revenue growth, CXW has the potential to return more value to its shareholders by maintaining financial discipline. That being said, investors should take note of the following factors that may affect my investment thesis.

Firstly, CXW had recently acquired Farmville Detention Center for $67 million, suggesting that the company is open to increasing capacity and meeting demand through acquisitions. As of now, a $67 million acquisition is still affordable and unlikely to affect its debt profile. However, if CXW engages in more M&A activities, there is a strong likelihood that its debt will start to increase. In such a situation, it is unlikely that we will continue to see margin expansion and returns through share repurchases.

Finally, there are currently multiple anti-ICE protests happening across the United States. Clearly, the general public is unhappy about the current administration's tough stance on immigration. Given that tariff policies have already been whipsawing, there is a possibility that the administration dial back on its immigration policies to appeal to the general public. If such a situation happens, we will not see a huge demand expansion, resulting in slower revenue growth or even revenue deterioration.

data.title: Western Union: Don't Fall For The 10% Dividend Yield
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recently wrote about International Money Express (IMXI), which dropped over 15% after its May 7th earnings call. About two weeks before the earnings call, I had initiated my SELL rating on Seeking Alpha, published here.

Western Union (NYSE:WU) follows a similar thesis to my original story on IMXI. At first glance, investors might be interested in WU for its 5x P/E ratio or the 10%+ dividend yield, but the stock is a classic value trap. Since 2013, Western Union’s gross profit has shrunk at a compound annual growth rate of over -3.6%. Recent industry trends, including the sector’s transition to digital remittances and the recent legislation changes, will erode WU’s top line and bottom line further. Despite my implied share price of $10.80 for the stock, I believe investors should avoid Western Union for its poor operational performance and regulatory headwinds. My investment rating, therefore, is a Sell.

Company Overview

Key Figures

Market Cap

$3.06B

5-Year Revenue Growth

-4.23%

Enterprise Value

$4.56B

Proj. Revenue Growth

-4.00%

12-month Return (%)

-27.94%

Dividend Yield

10.16%

Inside Ownership

0.41%

Price/Cash Flow

6.64x

Since Western Union’s 2007 IPO, the stock has been mostly flat before nosediving after 2021 for a total loss of -52.73% since inception.

Western Union’s stock price history accurately reflects its long-term gross profits. Looking at the table below from Macrotrends, the company’s gross profit has shrunk at a compound annual growth rate of -3.64% since its 2013 peak.

Operations

Western Union, operating since 1851, introduced its money transfer services in 1871 through its telegraph network. Today, Western Union is one of the largest remittance providers worldwide. The company transacts with more than 100 million customers in over 130 different currencies.

Western Union provides its remittance services through its retail agent network and through its digital platform. Its core business, and the majority of revenue, comes from the retail business, made up of over 380,000 agent locations.

As an example of how the transaction works, a customer in America could wire funds to a recipient (usually a friend or relative) in a country like Mexico. Western Union will charge a fee for providing the service and currency translation. In addition, the company will earn a portion of its revenue by capturing the difference in spread between the outbound and inbound currency exchange.

Industry

Transition to Digital

Remittance transactions have historically been conducted at retail locations. The leaders in the sector, like Western Union and MoneyGram (recently taken private), established their competitive advantage through extensive agent networks. As previously mentioned, Western Union operates with over 380k agents and operates in 200 countries which created significant barriers to entry.

However, more nimble competitors, like Remitly (RELY) and PagSeguro (PAGS), have been able to gain market share over Western Union through digital remittance offerings.

At the time of this publishing by Mastercard, the 2026 projected digital remittance volume is expected to be 61% while the cash remittance volume will make up only 39%.

Per the screenshot below from Western Union’s Q1 2025 earnings presentation, management recognizes the impact of building out its digital platform and has included it in its Evolve 2025 Strategy under the ‘Accelerate digital’ pillar.

Although Western Union has placed importance on its digital strategy over the last few years, several competitors with less operating history have more app downloads (shown in the table below).

Company

Total # of App Downloads

Remitly

3,059,622

Xoom

1,486,916

Wise

1,360,881

Western Union

1,317,129

MoneyGram

673,180

WorldRemit

421,476

Intermex

27,385

Remitly, briefly mentioned earlier, significantly leads competitors with over three million combined downloads. Paypal’s (PYPL) Xoom, Wise (OTCPK:WPLCF), and Western Union are all close seconds.

Although Western Union has a competitive number of app downloads, this has not translated to strong digital growth compared to peers.

Company

Years of Operating History

Year over Year Digital Growth

Remitly

14 Years

34.4%

Xoom

24 Years

9.0%

Wise

14 Years

16.5%

Western Union

174 Years

8.0%

Remitly and Wise have experienced the largest increase in digital growth, and the five-year story for the two companies is even greater. Remitly’s 5-year compound annual growth rate is 58.5% and Wise’s is 40.3%.

Although Western Union has devoted part of its 2025 strategy to growing its digital business, the company has significant competition in the space. Remitly and Wise both have less than two decades of operating experience but have been able to grow a large customer base and gain market share through their digital platform in a short period of time.

Regulatory Environment

During 2025, two notable policy changes have taken effect – and both will negatively impact Western Union. First, the current administration has proposed a 3.5% tax on remittances sent abroad by foreign workers, including green card holders and temporary visa workers. The tax will directly impact transaction volume, as the customer demographic is price-sensitive and generally lower-income migrant workers.

Additionally, the tax could negatively pressure margins on traditional retail agent-based networks like Western Union, MoneyGram, and International Money Express more than digital-only players like Wise or Remitly. The traditional agent-based network carries higher fixed costs than Wise or Remitly’s leaner, more adaptive cost structure.

Second, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau recently proposed an amendment to the way remittance servicers provide fee disclosures. The new rule is intended to promote transparency on fees and cost structure for users.

Per Moneyandbanking’s remittance fee graph, the average fee to transfer $200 is approximately 8% when customers use a money transfer operator (like Western Union). The amendment proposed by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau should increase transparency for customers. If customers have additional insight into the breakdown of their fees, they may be more likely to consider alternative offerings. This could drive down net remittance fees from the 8% average shown above, pressuring Western Union’s margins.

As an example of the enforcement on fee transparency, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau recently fined Wise $2.5M for “advertising inaccurate fees and failing to properly disclose exchange rates and other costs”.

Geopolitical Landscape

Over the last several years, geopolitical tension has increased substantially. Armed conflicts and economic sanctions – both of which affect Western Union’s business – have increased in regions most in need of remittance inflows.

Per the Global Change Data Lab’s World in Data Project, armed conflicts saw their smallest level of activity in 2010, at 86 total conflicts worldwide. Today, that number is 176 armed conflicts worldwide.

Western Union’s 2024 Annual Report calls out the Russia and Ukraine war as having an adverse effect on the business:

Demand for our services could soften, including due to low consumer confidence, high unemployment, high inflation, changes in foreign exchange rates, changes in monetary policy, reduced global trade, including from trade disruptions, trade restrictions, or tariffs, or other events, such as civil unrest, war, terrorism, natural disasters, including those related to climate change, public health emergencies or epidemics, and any changes arising as a result of the recent United States elections. For example, in March 2022, we suspended our operations in Russia and Belarus, due to the Russia/Ukraine conflict (the “Conflict”), which has had an adverse effect on our business, financial condition, results of operations, and cash flows. The Conflict has had and is expected to continue to have broader implications to our overall business, including reduced transaction activity in Ukraine.

Although the impact from the Russian invasion has led to only a small impairment charge on the Company’s income statement, the U.S. International Trade Commission estimates that remittance inflows to Russia could represent as much as the third-highest amount of total U.S. dollars sent to any country.

The International Trade Commission’s paper also highlights the sensitivity of remittances to countries with U.S.-led economic sanctions. As expected, countries with severe economic restrictions see reduced remittance inflows.

An example of Western Union’s sensitivity to countries with economic sanctions is the company’s Iraq business. In late 2023, the Central Bank of Iraq temporarily suspended Western Union’s international transfers in response to “know your customer” verification efforts.

Per the 2024 annual report, this negatively affected total company revenues by more than 3%.

With increasing geopolitical tensions – in the form of direct armed conflicts or economic sanctions – Western Union’s business will suffer. The revenue outlook will continue to look weak as markets most in need of remittance inflows will be the most affected by the tense global backdrop.

Performance

Full-Year Expectations

Western Union’s 2025 year to date performance continues to suffer from its reduced revenue in Iraq and the broader reduction in remittance outflows to South America. While the company captured a 7% quarter-over-quarter increase in its digital sales (on a non-adjusted basis), management affirmed 2025 guidance with full-year revenue down approximately 3%-4% and operating margins in the range of 18%-20%.

On a positive note, it’s worth mentioning that Western Union has embarked on a multi-year operational efficiency program intended to capture a total of $150M in cost savings.

In Q1 2025, the company achieved $30M in operational efficiencies. Western Union’s heavy retail-dependent business model creates high fixed costs compared to Wise and Remitly’s business model, so the Expense Redeployment Program should help offset some of those costs.

Proxy Statement and the Look Ahead

Previously shown in Macrotrends’ graph, gross profit has essentially shrunk at a compound annual growth rate of over -3.6%. But what’s most concerning for investors going forward is the management incentive structure related to growth, as outlined in the company’s proxy statement.

Per the screenshot below from the most recent proxy statement, the incentive structure is set up such that 25% of management’s bonus is tied to adjusted revenue growth and another 25% is tied to adjusted earnings per share.

The 2024 adjusted revenue growth target was -4%, meaning management actually beat its performance metric by shrinking the company’s revenue only 3% from its prior year. It’s important to keep in mind that this is an adjusted target – the revenue and earnings per share target does not even consider the asset impairment in Russia, acquisition and separation costs, and several other items management has deemed as one-off.

While investors have watched their 5-year investment in Western Union decrease more than 60%, management has significantly increased its personal compensation and payouts through an incentive structure misaligned with shareholders.

In this scenario, the proxy statement provides an important indicator for future growth expectations – investors should not expect the company to generate increases in revenue.

Peer Comparison

The table below includes Western Union’s top competitors, all of which have been previously mentioned.

In this scenario, I removed Western Union’s 2024 one-time tax benefit to normalize its financial, profitability, and valuation metrics.

From the table, investors will notice two important comparisons. First, WU is the only remittance operator on the list that has had negative growth over the last three years (and frankly, since its IPO). This is primarily due to WU’s competitors focusing on building out their digital services – something Western Union has not placed enough priority on.

The second important comparison is margins. Although from this table it appears that Western Union is in-line with peers, the digital-only providers still have a long runway for growth.

Because their cost structure better supports economies of scale as they attract new customers, I expect the group’s operating margin (excluding WU and IMXI) to hover closer to PAGS’ current margin of 33.3% in the future.

Valuation

To build out Western Union’s valuation model, my assumptions are based on notes from the proxy statement, historical operating performance, and management guidance.

As shown in the assumptions table above, I expect revenue to decline at -4% through my assumption period, which is the middle range for management’s 2025 guidance. Additionally, I have assumed the cost of sales and SG&A will be similar to historical operating performance.

I expect the cost of sales to increase by 50bps per year through to the terminal year due to the increasingly complex regulatory environment that Western Union will face. Specifically, as the company will be forced to switch its services to digital, WU will have higher variable costs per transaction, more investment in compliance, and a greater focus on cybersecurity to protect the integrity of its transactions.

Following the assumptions noted above, I have projected the 2025 operating income margin to be 19%, which is the mid-range of management’s estimate. I expect the margins to shrink over the next few years for the reasons related to digital service infrastructure noted above.

I arrive at an estimated price per share of $10.80, which is about 17.7% below the current share price. At first glance, this could imply Western Union is undervalued.

However, it’s important to note the significant risks when modeling a company in terminal decline. First, some of my estimates could be too conservative. For instance, management’s 2025 guidance range is -7% to +1% revenue growth. If the company were to come in on the lower range, towards -7% revenue decline, the valuation would be about 10% lower than where it is now.

Second, I have assumed a mostly fixed cost structure for the cost of sales and SG&A going forward. However, as previously mentioned, Western Union has high fixed cost from its retail agent locations. If revenues continue to decline at the current pace, and management doesn’t reduce its fixed cost structure, then its operating and net margin will erode faster than I have modeled for.

Conclusion

On the surface, Western Union looks like a great value opportunity. The company is trading at 5x its P/E (when including the one-time 2024 tax benefit) and has a current dividend yield over 10%. From my implied valuation model, the company even appears undervalued by approximately 17%. However, the company has significant risks that prevent it from being a good investment opportunity. Western Union is losing market share to competitors like Wise and Remitly, who are focused on building out their digital platform.

Additionally, the geopolitical tension and new legislation present further difficulties for Western Union's global operations. Finally, as shown in the proxy statement, management incentives are not aligned with shareholder value – and the incentive targets show investors that the company’s growth trajectory is headed towards terminal decline.

I recommend investors to sell Western Union stock given the exorbitant risks, poor operational performance, and increased regulatory headwinds.

data.title: War Scenarios: A Guide For Investors
data.description:
In the early morning hours of June 13, which was early evening in the US, Israel launched a series of preemptive strikes on Iran. This marks the start of a conflict that's likely to last several weeks, at minimum. The war could have profound implications for the global economy.

In this article, I will lay out scenarios that investors should contemplate as they plan how to manage their portfolios in response to this crisis.

A Review of Facts

Let us briefly review the facts as they stand.

1. Coordinated air, sea and land strikes. Israel has hit key Iranian targets with a series of precision aerial strikes, including piloted air strikes and unmanned strikes with missiles and drones. Attacks were also launched from the sea, including ships and submarines. In addition, attacks were carried out on the ground by Israeli commandos and intelligence operatives within Iran.

2. Decapitation. The strikes have been extremely successful in decapitating the entire top echelon of Iranian military leadership.

3. Key facilities struck. Initial attacks have focused on weakening Iranian defenses and retaliation capabilities by taking out air defense systems and missile launching systems.

4. Nuclear facilities hit. The Israelis have hit several sites that are key to Iran's nuclear program, but these hits have only been preliminary, as Iran's facilities are scattered all over the country and many are deep underground.

5. Iran has vowed revenge on both Israel and the US. Iranian officials have stated that they plan not only to carry out devastating attacks on Israel but that they also will hold the US responsible for enabling the strikes.

6. President Trump Supports Israel. Despite fierce criticism from many right-wing figures, President Trump called the strikes "excellent," and that there would be "more to come." And he has said that the US will not only protect itself if attacked but will protect Israel if called upon to do so. Trump has also said that Iran squandered an opportunity to negotiate a deal, but that the door is still open.

Scenarios for Investors

Despite the fact that investors will be confronted with a barrage of news connected with the war between Israel and Iran, investors only need to focus on a few things.

1. Closure Strait of Hormuz. By far, the most consequential potential event in this war, from the point of view of the global economy, would be if Iran decides to block passage of commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. As I have explained in detail in my article, "Oil Price Shock: The Path to $300," a blockage of the Strait of Hormuz would constitute a disruption in global oil supplies that's greater than that caused by the Arab Oil Embargo in 1973-1974. The relatively modest disruption at that time caused a quadrupling of global oil prices. A GAO study cited in that article estimates that a blockage of the Strait of Hormuz would cause a similar quadrupling of global oil prices. US generals have assessed that Iran does, indeed have the capacity to block the Strait of Hormuz. Furthermore, recent military actions by both Iran and the Houthis confirm that the possess the military capability to successfully stop virtually all traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. It's important to understand that Iran does not need to confront US naval vessels at sea. It will suffice that threaten to sink any ship that attempts to pass through the Strait. Under such conditions, no insurer will insure vessels passing through the Strait and no shipping company will allow its ships to pass through the Strait. For those interested in more details on the strategic military situation associated with the Strait of Hormuz, I cover this topic extensively in my SA blog post, "Iran Can Crash Global Markets: Blocking the Strait of Hormuz."

2. Attacks on oil facilities in the region. Iran has indicated that in the event of war they may choose to attack oil facilities in the region, including ports, production and processing facilities. Iran possesses the capability to inflict major damage on oil infrastructure in the Middle East through use of its drones, missiles and hypersonic missiles. In particular, air defenses at oil facilities in the Middle East do not have the capacity to withstand attacks from hypersonic missiles. Iran has previously shown a willingness to attack oil infrastructure in the region by having supported an armed Houthi attacks on the giant Abqaiq-Khurais facility in Saudi Arabia.

3. Attacks on US Military Facilities. Iran has previously threatened to attack US military facilities throughout the Middle East in the event of a conflict. Since Iran has held the US co-responsible for the strikes on Iran, it's likely that US military facilities, and possibly even civilian facilities, will be hit. If this is the case, this would draw the US more deeply into the war, with important economic implications, including a ballooning of US deficits as a result of increased military expenditures.

4. Activation of sleeper terrorist cells in the US. For many years it has been speculated that Iran possesses sleeper cells in the US that are tasked with carrying out terrorist attacks on US soil, upon command. While such reports are speculative, this possibility of terror attacks on US soil should not be discounted, as they are taken seriously by the FBI. Of particular concern to investors would be attacks on sensitive economically important targets and infrastructure.

What Should Investors Do?

In our view, the initial reaction of markets has grossly underestimated the potential economic and financial impact of the war. Initial spectacular Israeli successes have potentially lifted hopes that Iran's capacity to respond militarily have been so severely degraded that many of the risk listed above will not materialize.

In our view, risk is asymmetric. While everything could turn out fine and US equity markets could rally by 5%-10% on good news, the potential downside from this conflict for US equities is in the 30%-50% range.

In our view, investors would be wise to consider lowering risk in their portfolios. Investors should further consider capitalizing on this situation by increasing short-side bearish exposure to US equities and by investing in equities that could potentially benefit from the conflict.

Final Thoughts

At Successful Portfolio Strategy, we have been preparing for an Iran-Israel war for many months. We were positioned to profit from this war prior to the outset of this war and we will be making aggressive moves to capitalize on this situation in the days ahead, in accordance with our hour-by-hour assessment of developments.

data.title: Dollar Hits 3-Year-Low: Here's What It Means For Precious Metals (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

The U.S. dollar is undergoing a major technical breakdown—a highly bullish development for commodities, especially precious metals and their mining stocks.

For the past couple of months, I’ve been warning of a likely breakdown in the U.S. dollar—an event that historically signals a bullish turn for precious metals due to their well-established inverse relationship (read my recent report to learn more).

That breakdown is now unfolding, as I had anticipated, and gold, silver, and mining stocks are responding favorably. In this update, I’ll break down where these assets currently stand from a technical perspective and what’s likely to come next.

In my recent dollar updates, I emphasized the importance of the 100 level on the U.S. Dollar Index—a key support that had triggered several major rallies in recent years. But that support was recently broken, shifting my bias firmly bearish. In my latest analysis, I also pointed out the 98 level as the next important support and warned that a break below it would confirm a deeper selloff. Sure enough, the index sliced through that level today, hitting a three-year low and further reinforcing the downtrend.

The U.S. dollar has weakened over the past two days, driven by several key developments. Yesterday’s benign Consumer Price Index (CPI) report was followed by a soft Producer Price Index (PPI) reading today, reinforcing the disinflationary trend.

Adding to the pressure, a new report yesterday showed that U.S. continuing jobless claims have risen to their highest level in over three years. Together, these data points increase the likelihood of upcoming interest rate cuts—further weighing on the dollar’s outlook.

The weekly chart of the U.S. Dollar Index highlights how the 100 level has served as critical support for several years, with multiple rebounds occurring off of it. However, the recent decisive break below that level has given an important bearish signal. With that breakdown now validated, the next major support lies near the 90 level—which is now my next downside target.

Zooming out to the long-term monthly chart of the U.S. Dollar Index reveals that it has been trading within a rising channel for nearly two decades. A continued move toward the key 90 level would mark a decisive breakdown from that pattern—one that is likely to open the door to even deeper declines.

There are numerous fundamental reasons to remain bearish, including the dollar's extreme overvaluation relative to historical norms. A sustained dollar bear market would be like rocket fuel for commodities, especially precious metals and mining stocks. As a reminder, the early 2000s commodities bull market was fueled, in large part, by a prolonged dollar bear market—and I see strong parallels between that period and the current environment.

Next, let’s take a look at gold via COMEX futures, which I follow closely due to its tendency to trade in clean $100 increments—creating strong support and resistance around round numbers like $3,000, $3,100, $3,200, and so on.

Gold delivered a strong performance today, rising 1.76% on the back of soft inflation and jobs data, which pressured the U.S. dollar. Geopolitical tensions also contributed to the rally, with reports indicating that Israel may be weighing a strike on Iran’s nuclear facilities. This surge pushed gold futures up to the $3,400 level—but for a stronger bullish confirmation, I’d like to see a decisive breakout above that threshold in the days ahead.

As I’ve been showing in recent updates, gold remains in a healthy consolidation range between $3,200 and $3,500, pausing to catch its breath after strong gains earlier this spring.

This sideways action may continue a bit longer, but a decisive breakout above $3,500 is the key signal I’m watching. That would likely mark the start of gold’s next major leg higher—likely targeting $4,000 next, given its significance as a major psychological milestone that tends to act like a magnet for price.

It’s also worth noting that gold had been forming a bull flag pattern over the past couple of months—and it recently broke out to the upside. This is a technically bullish development that reinforces the strength of the current move and warrants close attention.

I’ve recently begun tracking gold priced in the World Currency Unit (WCU)—a composite currency based on the GDP-weighted average of the world’s 20 largest economies. In many ways, it offers one of the most balanced and accurate reflections of gold’s true global performance, which is why I’ve been paying close attention to it.

Gold priced in World Currency Units (WCUs) clearly demonstrates that its uptrend remains strong, with a well-defined trading range between 2,400 and 2,600 that’s important to watch closely:

Silver has finally broken through its long-standing $34–$35 resistance zone—a strong signal that its bull market is gaining momentum after a year of frustrating false starts. Despite a few quiet days, the breakout remains intact. In my view, silver is simply consolidating before its next leg higher—first to $40, and eventually much higher.

Next, let’s take a look at the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI)—a proprietary indicator I developed to help validate silver’s price action and filter out potential false moves. The SSPI is calculated as the average of gold and copper prices, with copper scaled by a factor of 540 to prevent gold from dominating the index. Interestingly, even though silver isn’t part of the calculation, the SSPI closely tracks its movements. To learn more, check out the recent article I published.

The SSPI remains in a consolidation range between 2,800 and 3,000. A breakout above the 3,000 level would be a strong bullish confirmation for silver. Recent bullish action in both gold and copper pushed the SSPI right up to that 3,000 mark—an encouraging sign—but I’m waiting for a decisive close above it to confirm the breakout. I’ll be watching this development closely and will keep you all updated as it unfolds.

I also like to examine the underlying components of the SSPI to get a clearer sense of what’s happening beneath the surface. Since we’ve already covered gold, let’s now take a quick look at copper.

Copper is just under a major resistance zone between $5.00 and $5.20. A breakout above that zone would likely mark the start of a new copper bull market—one that would lift the SSPI and, by extension, silver as well. That’s because algorithmic trading systems arbitrage price relationships across metals, often causing their movements to align.

Next, let’s look at gold mining stocks, as tracked by the popular large-cap VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX). Notably, GDX recently broke above the $42–$46 resistance zone I’ve been highlighting—a strong signal that gold miners are finally coming to life.

After years of being deeply undervalued, mining stocks are finally starting to attract attention and get bought up. I’m increasingly optimistic about the sector and will be writing more soon now that I have a much clearer read on their trajectory.

Silver miners, tracked by the SIL ETF, are also performing strongly and have entered the key $48–$52 resistance zone. A decisive breakout above this zone would provide further bullish confirmation—especially as silver’s momentum continues to build following its recent breakout.

To summarize, the U.S. dollar is undergoing a major technical breakdown, driven by signs of easing inflation and a weakening job market. Given the long-established inverse relationship between the dollar and commodities—especially precious metals—this breakdown is likely to serve as a powerful tailwind for gold, silver, and their mining stocks.

It’s an exciting and pivotal moment for the precious metals space, and I’m glad to have you on this journey with me.

Original Post
